- Each index is optimized for the morphological features of its language (plurals, possessives, etc.), for both documents and queries.

Each index contains all documents available on CanLII, across all languages. If CanLII has official translations, the index includes only the translation corresponding to the index's language.

    The Court upheld a decision finding the landlord strictly liable for rental unit deficiencies under the Residential Tenancies Act, rejecting arguments of due diligence and estoppel. The tenant’s rent abatement and unenforceability of certain lease clauses were also affirmed. The appeal was dismissed.



    AppealResidential tenancies





    **Lease** — Landlord obligations — Strict liability — Deficiencies in rental property — Landlord held strictly liable for deficiencies under section 49(1) of The Residential Tenancies Act, 2006 — Whether landlord’s lack of knowledge or due diligence absolves liability — Obligation to maintain habitable premises not contingent on landlord’s awareness — The Residential Tenancies Act, 2006, S.S. 2006, c. R-22.0001, s. 49(1)



    **Lease** — Security deposit — Estoppel — Landlord’s claim to tenant’s security deposit barred by estoppel — Representation made by landlord to tenant regarding claims — Tenant relied on representation to her detriment — Whether hearing officer erred in applying estoppel test — The Residential Tenancies Act, 2006, S.S. 2006, c. R-22.0001, s. 33



    **Lease** — Mitigation of damages — Tenant’s duty to mitigate — Tenant found to have acted reasonably to minimize damages — Standard conditions not included in written tenancy agreement — Whether tenant’s actions satisfied statutory duty to mitigate — The Residential Tenancies Act, 2006, S.S. 2006, c. R-22.0001, s. 8(2)



    **Contracts** — Enforceability of tenancy clauses — Late payment charge and carpet cleaning clause — Late payment charge of $5 per day found to be an unenforceable penalty — Carpet cleaning clause void for inconsistency with tenant’s obligation to leave unit reasonably clean — Whether clauses violated sections 7(2) and 51(a) of The Residential Tenancies Act, 2006 — The Residential Tenancies Act, 2006, S.S. 2006, c. R-22.0001, ss. 7(2), 51(a)



    **Lease** — Rent abatement — Tenant awarded $2,500 rent abatement — Landlord’s breach of section 49(1) of The Residential Tenancies Act, 2006 — Hearing officer’s discretion in determining quantum of abatement — Whether award was manifestly unreasonable — The Residential Tenancies Act, 2006, S.S. 2006, c. R-22.0001, s. 49(1)

    1995-05-16  \|  24 pages  \|  cited by [306 documents](https://www.canlii.org/en/#search/origin1=/en/on/onca/doc/1995/1995canlii959/1995canlii959.html)



    AI-generated

    The Court ruled that a public sale of a family business was unjust and punitive. Instead, the appropriate remedy was for the family to purchase the ousted shareholder's shares at fair market value without a minority discount, ensuring rectification of oppression while respecting family dynamics and shareholder expectations.



    Business





    **Business associations** — Oppression remedy — Family business — Section 248 of the Ontario Business Corporations Act (OBCA) — Father retained control of business despite gifting equity to sons — Son excluded from business operations and income — What is the appropriate remedy for oppressive conduct in a family business? — Remedy must rectify oppression without exceeding reasonable expectations or being punitive — OBCA, R.S.O. 1990, c. B.16, s. 248



    **Statutory interpretation** — Oppression remedy — Scope of section 248 of the OBCA — Public sale of family business ordered by trial judge — Whether public sale exceeds the statutory purpose of rectifying oppression — Court held that remedies under section 248 must rectify oppression and not punish — OBCA, R.S.O. 1990, c. B.16, s. 248



    **Family** — Oppression in family business — Intersection of family dynamics and corporate law — Whether oppression remedy can address personal interests arising from family relationships — Court held that section 248 protects only corporate interests as shareholder, director, or officer — Family disputes cannot justify oppressive conduct — OBCA, R.S.O. 1990, c. B.16, s. 248



    **Securities** — Share valuation — Oppression remedy — Minority discount — Whether minority discount should apply in valuing shares for oppression remedy — Court held that fair market value of shares must exclude minority discount to ensure equitable compensation — OBCA, R.S.O. 1990, c. B.16, s. 248







    \[…\]   Naneff v. Con-Crete **Holdings** Limited et al. \[Indexed as: Naneff v. Con-Crete **Holdings** Ltd.\] 23 O.R. (3d) 481   \[…\]  Except where it becomes necessary to refer to specific details of the companies and their **holdings**, I will refer to them comprehensively as the business, or the family business.   \[…\]   I would think that this principle would **hold** true even if the past conduct of the oppressor were found to be scandalous.   \[…\]

    **Civil procedure** — Costs — Discretion of the court — Departure from the general rule that costs follow the event — Plaintiff ordered to pay costs to Mrs. Purvis on a complete indemnity basis despite being successful in part — No costs awarded to Plaintiff against Mrs. Misek — Principles governing costs in cases of misconduct and unnecessary litigation — Courts of Justice Act, R.S.O. 1990, c. C.43, s. 131(1); Rules of Civil Procedure, R.R.O. 1990, Reg. 194, Rule 57.01



    **Property** — Prescriptive easements — Costs — Plaintiff sought declaration that Defendant’s property did not enjoy a prescriptive easement — Plaintiff’s action against Mrs. Purvis dismissed — Costs awarded to Mrs. Purvis on a complete indemnity basis due to unnecessary litigation and improper conduct — No costs awarded to Plaintiff against Mrs. Misek despite her unsuccessful claim — Principles of fairness and proportionality applied



    **Professional responsibility** — Costs against lawyers — Rule 57.07 — Request to hold Plaintiff’s lawyers jointly and severally liable for costs awarded to Mrs. Purvis — Court declined to impose liability on lawyers absent egregious misconduct — Rule 57.07 requires extreme caution in awarding costs against counsel — Rules of Civil Procedure, R.R.O. 1990, Reg. 194, Rule 57.07



    **Statutory interpretation** — Costs principles — Modern costs rules — Purposes of costs awards include indemnification, discouraging frivolous claims, and encouraging settlements — Exceptional circumstances required for complete indemnity costs — Misconduct and unnecessary litigation as factors justifying departure from general costs rules — Courts of Justice Act, R.S.O. 1990, c. C.43, s. 131(1); Rules of Civil Procedure, R.R.O. 1990, Reg. 194, Rule 57.01

    The Court upheld the Securities Commission's cease-trade order, finding the take-over bid abusive and contrary to public interest, even without a breach of the Securities Act. The Commission acted within its jurisdiction to protect market integrity and shareholders, emphasizing its discretion under public interest provisions. Appeal dismissed.\
\
\
\
    Commerce and industry\
\
\
\
\
\
    **Securities** — Cease-trade orders — Public interest — Ontario Securities Commission issuing cease-trade order on take-over bid and controlling shareholders' trades — Did the Commission err in failing to specify a time period for the order? — Securities Act, R.S.O. 1980, c. 466, s. 123 — Cease-trade orders valid for the duration of the bid unless otherwise specified\
\
\
\
    **Administrative law** — Jurisdiction — Ontario Securities Commission — Cease-trade order issued under section 123 of the Securities Act based on public interest — Does the Commission have jurisdiction to act absent a concurrent breach of the Act, regulations, or policy statements? — Broad discretion under section 123 to act in the public interest\
\
\
\
    **Administrative law** — Market abuse — Take-over bid — Ontario Securities Commission finding bid abusive of market and Class A shareholders — Was the bid structured to circumvent the "coat-tail" provision and avoid protections for Class A shareholders? — Evidence supported finding of abuse and public interest violation\
\
\
\
    **Evidence** — Admissibility — Intention behind corporate reorganization — Evidence of intention in 1983 reorganization and "coat-tail" provision admitted to assess public interest impact of take-over bid — Was such evidence admissible and relevant? — Evidence admissible under Statutory Powers Procedure Act, R.S.O. 1980, c. 484, s. 15\
\
\
\
    **Constitution** — Section 96 courts — Ontario Securities Commission — Did the Commission's actions under section 123 of the Securities Act violate section 96 of the Constitution Act, 1867, by exercising powers exclusive to superior courts? — Commission's powers under section 123 upheld as regulatory, not judicial\
\
\
\
\

Instead of amalgamating the two companies, Copthorne I sold its VHHC **Holdings** shares to Big City, in order to avoid the vertical amalgamation and cancellation of the PUC of the shares of VHHC **Holdings**.   \[…\]\

metadata.description: CanLII is a non-profit organization managed by the Federation of Law Societies of Canada. CanLII's goal is to make Canadian law accessible for free on the Internet. This website provides access to court judgments, tribunal decisions, statutes and regulations from all Canadian jurisdictions.
